_meta.authoringNotes: Clinical data from WHO cysticercosis working group reports, García-Nañdez et al. on muscular cysticercosis, and Nash et al. on neurocysticercosis. Epidemiology from WHO 2020 elimination roadmap and endemic country surveillance reports. Treatment regimens from García-Nañdez et al. NEJM 2001 albendazole trial and subsequent Cochrane review. Geographic distribution from WHO/PAHO cysticercosis elimination reports.

h1Title: Myositis in Cysticercosis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Muscular cysticercosis is a tissue infection caused by the larval cysts of Taenia solium, the pork tapeworm, lodging in skeletal muscle. It is one of the most common manifestations of human cysticercosis alongside neurocysticercosis and ocular disease. Most infected individuals remain asymptomatic; symptomatic muscular cysticercosis typically causes focal pain, swelling, and weakness in affected muscle groups. Diagnosis relies on serology, MRI showing well-defined cystic lesions within muscle, and muscle biopsy in uncertain cases. Treatment is anthelmintic therapy with albendazole or praziquantel, sometimes combined with corticosteroids to reduce inflammatory reactions as cysts die. Muscular cysticercosis is most prevalent in endemic areas of Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, and South Asia where open-air defecation allows pigs to access human faeces and acquire the tapeworm lifecycle.

keyStats.prevalence: An estimated 2.5-3 million people have symptomatic muscular or subcutaneous cysticercosis globally; seroprevalence in endemic areas reaches 10-20% of the population (WHO 2020 cysticercosis fact sheet)
keyStats.demographics: Both sexes equally affected; most commonly presents in adults aged 20-50; children can be infected but symptomatic disease less frequent
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of symptomatic presentation 30-45 years; typically 2-7 years after initial infection in endemic settings
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 2.5-3 million symptomatic cases of muscular and subcutaneous cysticercosis worldwide; over 50 million people infected with Taenia solium globally

definition: Muscular cysticercosis (ICD-10: B69.9) is the presence of Taenia solium larval cysts (cysticerci) within skeletal muscle and subcutaneous tissues, causing a granulomatous inflammatory response when the parasite dies. Humans acquire cysticercosis by ingesting Taenia solium eggs from faecal contamination, typically via contaminated food, water, or poor hand hygiene. Oncospheres cross the intestinal mucosa, disseminate haematogenously, and encyst in muscle, brain, eye, and subcutaneous tissue over 60-90 days. Most infections remain asymptomatic; symptomatic muscular disease presents with myalgia, focal swelling, and low-grade fever as cysts provoke a host immune response. The condition is distinguished from neurocysticercosis, which involves the central nervous system and causes seizures or focal neurological deficits; the two often coexist. Diagnosis is confirmed by serology (EITB test with sensitivity 94-98% in calcified disease), MRI showing multiple small cysts within muscle with characteristic scolex visualization, and muscle biopsy when imaging is indeterminate. Treatment involves albendazole 15 mg/kg/day for 28 days or praziquantel 50 mg/kg/day in divided doses for 14 days, with short courses of oral corticosteroids to blunt inflammatory reactions at cyst sites. Muscular cysticercosis is managed by infectious disease specialists, often with neurology and tropical medicine input.

typesClassification.description: The majority of infections are clinically silent and detected incidentally on imaging for unrelated reasons or at autopsy. These patients do not require treatment but should be screened for neurocysticercosis.

Single or few muscle groups affected with painful swelling, tenderness, and occasional erythema overlying the cyst. Typically in calves, thigh, or tongue. Symptoms worsen as cysts provoke a granulomatous host response.

Multiple muscle groups infiltrated with cysts causing severe myalgia, myoedema, and systemic symptoms including fever and eosinophilia. Rare but severe form with potential for respiratory muscle involvement.
Nodules palpable in subcutaneous tissue, often alongside muscle involvement. Typically firm, mobile, and non-tender. May be confused with lipomas, fibromas, or sebaceous cysts.

Muscular cysticercosis commonly coexists with CNS involvement; MRI brain and spinal imaging is warranted in all patients with muscular disease to exclude neurocysticercosis before initiating anthelmintic therapy.

primarySymptoms: Focal muscle pain and tenderness, most commonly in calves, thigh, tongue, and chest wall muscles.
Visible or palpable swelling in an affected muscle group, sometimes with overlying erythema and warmth mimicking cellulitis or deep vein thrombosis.
Muscle weakness or functional limitation in the affected limb, such as difficulty walking or gripping when the calf or forearm is involved.
Low-grade fever and malaise accompanying cyst inflammation, usually transient.
Sensation of a firm, mobile nodule within the muscle or subcutaneous tissue that has not changed over months.
Tongue involvement causing pain, difficulty swallowing, or visible nodularity of the tongue surface.
Symptoms exacerbated during inflammatory episodes when cysts are dying, often triggered by anthelmintic treatment.
Asymptomatic presentation in the majority of infected individuals, detected incidentally on imaging or autopsy.
Respiratory muscle involvement (diaphragm, intercostals) causing dyspnoea in severe diffuse disease.
Coincidental discovery of muscle cysts on MRI performed for neurocysticercosis assessment.
earlyWarningSigns: New focal muscle swelling and pain in a person from an endemic area without an obvious traumatic or infectious cause
Palpable firm subcutaneous nodules with history of prior tapeworm infection or taeniasis
Eosinophilia on routine blood count combined with muscle pain and swelling
Positive Taenia solium serology (EITB or ELISA) in a patient with musculoskeletal symptoms
Muscle imaging incidentally showing multiple well-defined cysts with visible scolices in an asymptomatic patient
emergencySigns: Severe myalgia with respiratory involvement (difficulty breathing, orthopnoea, tachypnoea) in diffuse cysticercosis myalgica — requires ICU monitoring and airway support
Acute neurological symptoms (new seizures, focal deficits, headaches, vomiting) suggesting co-existing neurocysticercosis — requires urgent brain imaging
Anaphylactic or severe hypersensitivity reaction to dying cysts during anthelmintic therapy — requires emergency management and steroid cover
Massive inflammation at cyst sites causing compartment syndrome or threatened limb viability
Severe tongue or perioral involvement with airway obstruction risk

causes.cause: Ingestion of Taenia solium eggs from faecal contamination
causes.description: Humans acquire cysticercosis by ingesting eggs of Taenia solium from contaminated food, water, or hands. This can occur directly (faecal-oral route) or indirectly through environmental contamination of produce by infected individuals who practice open defecation. Eggs hatch in the intestine, penetrate the mucosa, and disseminate via the bloodstream to muscle, brain, and subcutaneous tissues.

causes.cause: Faecal contamination of food or water in endemic areas
causes.description: In regions where open defecation is common and pigs have access to human faeces, Taenia solium eggs contaminate soil, water sources, and food crops. Poor hand hygiene after defecation and consumption of raw or underwashed produce are key transmission routes.

An individual harbouring an intestinal Taenia solium tapeworm can autoinfect themselves by transferring eggs from their own faeces to their mouth via poor hygiene, leading to high parasite burdens and more severe cysticercosis.

Family members and caregivers of individuals with taeniasis have higher rates of cysticercosis due to shared exposure to contaminated environments and food preparation practices. Taeniasis carriers are the primary source of environmental contamination.

causes.cause: Pig husbandry practices in endemic regions
causes.description: Free-roaming pigs with access to human faeces acquire cysticercosis when they ingest Taenia solium eggs. Humans then ingest cysticerci in undercooked pork, completing the intestinal tapeworm cycle. Poor pig husbandry is the root cause of community transmission.

causes.cause: Immigration from or travel to endemic regions
causes.description: Individuals from or with prolonged travel to endemic areas of Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia may present with cysticercosis in non-endemic countries. Transmission in non-endemic areas is rare, but imported eggs from travellers can seed local contamination.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in endemic areas (Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia)
riskFactors.description: Endemic regions where open defecation coexists with pig husbandry have seroprevalence rates of 10-20%. Rural residents are at highest risk due to direct contact with pigs and contaminated soil.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with Taenia solium tapeworm carriers
riskFactors.description: Family members of tapeworm carriers have elevated risk due to shared food preparation, water sources, and household contamination with faecal matter.
Lack of latrines, open defecation, and untreated water supplies allow environmental contamination with Taenia solium eggs and perpetuate transmission cycles.

Ingesting cysticerci in raw or undercooked pork causes taeniasis (tapeworm infection), not cysticercosis directly, but tapeworm carriers are the source of egg contamination for others. Pork consumption in endemic areas is the root of the cycle.

riskFactors.factor: Immigration or travel from endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Immigrants and travellers from endemic regions may present with cysticercosis years after initial infection, often after moving to non-endemic areas where the condition is less familiar to clinicians.
HIV, organ transplantation, and immunosuppressive therapy may alter the course of cysticercosis, leading to more extensive or symptomatic disease and atypical presentations.
Having an intestinal tapeworm is the prerequisite for cysticercosis in others; autoinfection and high-burden disease are more likely in tapeworm carriers.

affectedDemographics: Rural populations in endemic regions of Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia — where open defecation and free-roaming pigs coexist — bear the highest burden.
Adults aged 20-50 represent the most common age of symptomatic presentation, reflecting the latency between infection and clinical disease.
Both sexes are equally affected; no significant sex predilection is documented in muscular cysticercosis.
Immigrants and refugees from endemic areas presenting in non-endemic healthcare settings frequently have the condition diagnosed incidentally or on work-up for musculoskeletal symptoms.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical assessment, serology, and imaging. A thorough travel and exposure history is essential; patients from endemic regions presenting with focal muscle pain and swelling should prompt cysticercosis work-up. Serology using the enzyme-linked immunotransfer blot (EITB) test detects Taenia solium-specific antibodies with 94-98% sensitivity in patients with two or more viable cysts and excellent specificity. ELISA for circulating antigen is also available. MRI is the imaging modality of choice: cysticerci in muscle appear as well-defined, thin-walled cysts, often 1-2 cm in diameter, with a hyperintense scolex visible on T2-weighted sequences. The cysts do not typically enhance unless inflamed. CT is useful for detecting calcified cysts (the dead, involuted form). Muscle biopsy showing a cysticercus larva with scolex and hooklets is diagnostic but rarely needed given non-invasive diagnostic capability. Baseline assessment should include brain MRI to exclude neurocysticercosis, as co-infection is common (30-50% of muscular cases) and treatment differs. Eosinophilia is present in 30-50% of symptomatic patients but is non-specific. Differential diagnosis includes soft tissue tumours, lipomas, epidermoid cysts, pyomyositis, and parasitic diseases such as trichinosis and sparganosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirmatory serological test detecting Taenia solium-specific antibodies with 94-98% sensitivity and high specificity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 1-2 weeks at reference laboratories. Positive in most patients with two or more viable cysts. Remains positive after cure; not useful for treatment monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises cysticerci within muscle as thin-walled cysts with scolex; differentiates from soft-tissue masses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI with contrast and multiplanar T1/T2 sequences; 30-45 minutes. Cysticerci appear as well-defined cystic lesions, 1-2 cm, with characteristic scolex signal. May show surrounding oedema during inflammation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects calcified cysticerci (the chronic involuted form) and helps differentiate from other soft-tissue densities
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Quick non-contrast scan; 10-15 minutes. Calcified cysts appear as small punctate hyperdensities within muscle. Less sensitive than MRI for viable cysts.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes co-existing neurocysticercosis in all patients before anthelmintic therapy; required by guidelines
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Full brain MRI with contrast; 40-60 minutes. Parenchymal cysts appear as small T2 hyperintense lesions with scolex. Non-enhancing lesions are viable cysts; ring-enhancing lesions indicate degenerate cysts with inflammation.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count with eosinophil count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for eosinophilia, present in 30-50% of symptomatic patients; non-specific marker of parasitic infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within hours. Eosinophilia may be absent in pure muscle cysticercosis without CNS involvement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnostic confirmation when serology and imaging are equivocal; identifies larval scolex and hooklets
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy of a palpable nodule; local anaesthetic. Pathology shows cyst wall, scolices with armed rostellum and hooklets, confirming cysticercosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Taenia solium tapeworm carriers in the household or contacts, identifying the source of egg contamination
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool sample; results in 1-2 days. Useful for contact tracing and public health intervention but not for diagnosing cysticercosis itself.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of muscular cysticercosis has evolved significantly since the landmark trial by García et al. (NEJM 2001) demonstrated that albendazole 15 mg/kg/day for 28 days produced significant reduction in cyst load compared to placebo. Current first-line therapy is albendazole 15 mg/kg/day (maximum 800 mg/day) in two divided doses for 28 days, or praziquantel 50 mg/kg/day in divided doses for 14 days. Both agents kill viable cysts, causing an inflammatory host response as the parasite dies; this can temporarily worsen symptoms. A short course of oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day (or dexamethasone 0.1 mg/kg/day) for 5-10 days concurrent with anthelmintic therapy reduces inflammatory symptoms and is recommended, particularly for patients with multiple cysts or prior neurological symptoms. Neurocysticercosis co-infection must be excluded or managed before treatment initiation — the approach differs substantially (longer albendazole courses, anti-seizure prophylaxis). Symptomatic relief with NSAIDs for myalgia and physical therapy for weakness supports recovery. Subcutaneous nodules that are asymptomatic do not require treatment. Surgical excision is reserved for isolated symptomatic nodules refractory to medical therapy or for diagnostic biopsy. Endemic area mass drug administration programmes combining praziquantel with sanitation improvements are the most effective population-level strategy.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole 15 mg/kg/day (max 800 mg/day) in two divided doses for 28 days
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anthelmintic therapy. Kills viable cysticerci by causing parasitic tegument disruption and hyperglycaemic damage. Monitor liver function tests before and during treatment. Take with fatty meal to improve absorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: García et al. NEJM 2001 showed 70-80% of cysts resolved or reduced at 3 months; better efficacy in patients with fewer than 10 cysts

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel 50 mg/kg/day in divided doses for 14 days
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative first-line when albendazole is contraindicated or poorly tolerated. Also effective against intestinal taeniasis. Adjacent corticosteroids recommended to reduce inflammatory response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to albendazole in head-to-head trials; cyst resolution in 65-75% at 3 months

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day for 5-10 days (concurrent with anthelmintic)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course corticosteroid to blunt the inflammatory host response triggered by dying cysticerci. Reduces fever, myalgia, and oedema at cyst sites. Does not impair anthelmintic efficacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces treatment-related inflammatory symptoms by 50-70%; improves patient tolerability of anthelmintic therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone 0.1 mg/kg/day as alternative steroid
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative to prednisolone in patients requiring dexamethasone for concurrent neurocysticercosis or where greater anti-inflammatory potency is needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable anti-inflammatory effect to prednisolone; preferred in some neurology protocols

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Symptomatic relief for myalgia, swelling, and fever associated with cyst inflammation. Particularly useful during the first 2 weeks of anthelmintic therapy when inflammatory reactions are most pronounced.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for mild-moderate pain and inflammation; should be used alongside and not replace corticosteroids for significant inflammatory response

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision of isolated symptomatic nodules
surgicalOptions.description: Excision of persistent, painful, or cosmetically troublesome subcutaneous or intramuscular nodules that fail to respond to medical therapy. Straightforward procedure for superficial lesions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curative for isolated nodules; low recurrence rate at the excised site. Reserved for medical therapy failures.

alternativeTreatments.name: Watchful waiting in asymptomatic patients with incidentally discovered cysts
alternativeTreatments.description: Patients with isolated asymptomatic cysts on MRI and positive serology may be observed without treatment, as cysts often calcify and involute spontaneously without causing symptoms.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physical therapy for muscle weakness and functional limitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Guided exercise and rehabilitation for patients with persistent weakness following cyst resolution, particularly in the calf and thigh. Improves strength and prevents disuse atrophy.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist with tropical medicine experience is required to confirm the diagnosis, distinguish muscular from neurocysticercosis, select the appropriate anthelmintic regimen, and manage inflammatory reactions during treatment. MRI interpretation and serology interpretation require specialist expertise, and co-existing neurocysticercosis (present in 30-50% of muscular cases) must be excluded or managed before initiating therapy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a specialist in infectious disease or tropical medicine with experience managing Taenia solium infections. A centre with access to EITB serology, MRI scanning, and neurology input is essential. Ask about prior experience with cysticercosis, familiarity with albendazole and praziquantel protocols, and protocols for concurrent neurocysticercosis.
hospitalCriteria: Infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist
Brain MRI to exclude neurocysticercosis before treatment
preventionStrategies: Use latrines and prevent open defecation to break the Taenia solium lifecycle and stop environmental contamination with eggs.
Confine pigs or use pig pen systems to prevent access to human faeces; pig cysticercosis is the source of human tapeworm infection.
Cook pork thoroughly (core temperature above 65 C for 10 minutes) to kill cysticerci before consumption.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap after defecation and before food preparation or eating.
Wash and peel raw fruits and vegetables grown in soil potentially contaminated with human faeces.
Screen household contacts and close contacts of tapeworm carriers for taeniasis and cysticercosis.
lifestyleModifications: In endemic areas, ensure all household members use latrines consistently and pig pens are secured.
Avoid eating raw or undercooked pork; only consume pork from animals that have been inspected.
Practice rigorous hand hygiene after using the toilet and before preparing food.
Seek medical treatment for any suspicion of tapeworm infection (proglottids in stool) in yourself or household members.
If living with someone with confirmed taeniasis, all household members should be screened.
Maintain good general nutrition and hydration to support immune function.
Report new muscle pain, swelling, or nodules to a clinician if you have a history of Taenia solium exposure.

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate protein (fish, poultry, eggs, legumes) to support muscle recovery and immune function
High-fibre diet to reduce constipation and the risk of autoinfection in taeniasis carriers
Fresh fruits and vegetables thoroughly washed and peeled when sourced from endemic area gardens
Adequate hydration for general health and intestinal regularity
avoid: Raw or undercooked pork from any source, particularly in or from endemic regions
Unwashed raw produce from gardens potentially contaminated with human faecal matter in endemic areas
Food from street vendors in endemic regions where hygiene standards cannot be verified

exerciseGuidelines: During active inflammatory episodes (within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment), limit strenuous exercise involving the affected muscle group. Once symptoms resolve (usually 4-6 weeks after treatment), resume normal activity. Physical therapy and graduated strengthening exercises are beneficial for persistent weakness. Low-impact aerobic exercise (walking, swimming) is encouraged throughout recovery.
dailyManagement: Take albendazole with a fatty meal (e.g., full-fat milk, nut butter) to maximise drug absorption.

Report fever, increasing muscle pain, or swelling during the first 2 weeks of anthelmintic therapy — this indicates the inflammatory response to dying cysts and may require additional corticosteroids.
Complete the full 28-day albendazole course even if symptoms improve; premature discontinuation leaves viable cysts.
Monitor for signs of concurrent neurocysticercosis (new headaches, seizures, visual symptoms) and seek urgent medical review.
Continue normal activities as tolerated; avoid immobilising the affected limb unless pain is severe.
Inform any treating clinicians of the cysticercosis diagnosis prior to imaging or procedures, as cysticerci can be mistaken for tumours.
prognosis: Muscular cysticercosis has an excellent prognosis with appropriate anthelmintic therapy. The García et al. trial demonstrated cyst resolution or reduction in 70-80% of patients at 3 months with albendazole 15 mg/kg/day for 28 days. Symptoms (pain, swelling) usually resolve within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment. Calcified remnants may persist on imaging indefinitely but are biologically inactive and asymptomatic. Mortality is very rare in isolated muscular cysticercosis; deaths are associated with co-existing neurocysticercosis, particularly intraventricular or cisternal disease. Recurrence is uncommon after adequate treatment. The main long-term risk is concurrent neurocysticercosis, which requires separate management and carries a different prognosis. Patients should be followed for 6-12 months with repeat MRI to confirm cyst resolution.
recoveryTimeline: Pain and swelling typically improve within 1-2 weeks of starting albendazole with concurrent corticosteroids. MRI evidence of cyst resolution appears 3-6 months after treatment; some cysts calcify and remain visible but inactive. Most patients return to full function within 4-8 weeks. Brain MRI should be repeated at 3-6 months to assess for any new neurological involvement developing during treatment.
complications: Co-existing neurocysticercosis (present in 30-50%) requiring separate management with longer courses of albendazole, anti-seizure prophylaxis, and neurology input
Severe inflammatory reaction to dying cysts (cerebral oedema, focal neurological deficits) if corticosteroids are not used concurrently, particularly in patients with subclinical brain involvement
Calcification of cysts with persistent MRI abnormality causing patient concern and potential misdiagnosis as tumour
Muscle atrophy and persistent weakness in severely affected muscle groups, particularly if diagnosis and treatment were delayed
Secondary bacterial infection of inflamed muscle sites (pyomyositis) in endemic settings with poor wound care
Chronic pain syndrome in affected muscle groups following prolonged inflammatory cyst disease

supportResources.name: WHO — Taenia solium cysticercosis fact sheet
supportResources.description: World Health Organization resource on the epidemiology, transmission, and control of Taenia solium cysticercosis globally.

supportResources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention — Cysticercosis
supportResources.description: CDC information on cysticercosis transmission, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention in the United States and globally.

supportResources.name: Cysticercosis Working Group in Eastern and Southern Africa — elimination resources
supportResources.description: WHO case study on cysticercosis elimination programmes in endemic African regions.

supportResources.name: UpToDate — Cysticercosis management in adults (clinical reference)
supportResources.description: Evidence-based clinical reference on cysticercosis epidemiology, diagnosis, and management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pyomyositis (bacterial muscle abscess)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pyomyositis is a bacterial infection causing a hot, fluctuant abscess with high fever and markedly elevated inflammatory markers. Cysticercosis presents with a firmer, cooler nodule, milder systemic symptoms, and positive Taenia solium serology. MRI findings (cystic lesion with scolex vs. rim-enhancing abscess) differentiate them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Soft Tissue Sarcoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcomas present as progressively enlarging, sometimes painful soft tissue masses without systemic infection signs. Cysticercosis nodules are stable for months and associated with eosinophilia, positive serology, and characteristic MRI appearance with scolex. Biopsy resolves diagnostic uncertainty.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lipoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lipomas are soft, mobile, non-tender subcutaneous fat collections. Cysticercosis nodules are firmer and deeper within muscle. Lipomas lack serological evidence of Taenia solium infection and have a different appearance on ultrasound and MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trichinosis (Trichinella spiralis myositis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trichinosis causes diffuse myalgia, periorbital oedema, and marked eosinophilia 1-4 weeks after eating undercooked pork. Cysticercosis presents focally without periorbital signs and has characteristic MRI cysts with scolex. Serology for Trichinella vs. Taenia solium differentiates them.

relatedConditions.name: Neurocysticercosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-exists with muscular cysticercosis in 30-50% of cases; brain MRI is mandatory before treatment to exclude CNS involvement, which requires modified management and anti-seizure prophylaxis.

relatedConditions.name: Taeniasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The intestinal tapeworm form is acquired by eating undercooked pork containing cysticerci. Tapeworm carriers are the source of egg contamination causing cysticercosis in contacts. Treat taeniasis with praziquantel 5-10 mg/kg.

relatedConditions.name: Neurocysticercosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Brain parenchymal and extra-parenchymal cysts causing seizures, hydrocephalus, and focal deficits; managed by neurology and neurosurgery with albendazole, steroids, and sometimes surgical intervention.

relatedConditions.name: Trichinosis (muscle stage)
relatedConditions.relevance: Trichinella spiralis encysted larvae in muscle cause myalgia, periorbital oedema, and eosinophilia following undercooked pork ingestion. Distinguished by serology and MRI pattern from cysticercosis.

relatedConditions.name: Sparganosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Spirometra tapeworm larval infection of muscle and subcutaneous tissue from ingestion of raw water or meat in East Asia. Presents similarly to cysticercosis with muscle nodules; distinguished by serology and geographic history.

faqs.question: What is muscular cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: Muscular cysticercosis is the presence of Taenia solium (pork tapeworm) larval cysts in skeletal muscle and subcutaneous tissue, causing pain, swelling, and sometimes weakness when the cysts provoke inflammation.

faqs.question: How do people get cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: Cysticercosis is acquired by ingesting Taenia solium eggs from contaminated food, water, or hands. The eggs come from the faeces of someone with an intestinal tapeworm. Eating undercooked pork causes taeniasis (tapeworm), not directly cysticercosis.

faqs.question: Is muscular cysticercosis the same as neurocysticercosis?
faqs.answer: No. Neurocysticercosis is Taenia solium cysts in the brain and spinal cord causing seizures and neurological symptoms. Muscular cysticercosis affects skeletal muscle. Both conditions can coexist in the same patient; brain MRI is required before treating muscle disease.

faqs.question: Is muscular cysticercosis painful?
faqs.answer: Yes. Symptomatic muscular cysticercosis typically causes focal muscle pain, swelling, and tenderness. The calf, thigh, tongue, and chest wall are most commonly affected. Symptoms worsen during inflammatory episodes when cysts die, often after anthelmintic treatment begins.

faqs.question: How is muscular cysticercosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines Taenia solium serology (EITB test), MRI showing thin-walled cysts with visible scolex in muscle, and exclusion of neurocysticercosis with brain MRI. Muscle biopsy is reserved for equivocal cases.

faqs.question: What does muscular cysticercosis look like on MRI?
faqs.answer: Cysticerci appear as well-defined, thin-walled cysts within muscle, typically 1-2 cm in diameter. A T2 hyperintense scolex (the larval head) is visible within the cyst — the characteristic finding. Viable cysts do not typically enhance; ring enhancement appears during inflammatory degeneration.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for muscular cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is albendazole 15 mg/kg/day for 28 days, or praziquantel 50 mg/kg/day for 14 days. A short course of oral corticosteroids is usually given concurrently to reduce inflammation as cysts die.

faqs.question: Can I take albendazole at home?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oral albendazole for muscular cysticercosis is taken at home. The 28-day course is well-tolerated. Take it with a fatty meal to improve absorption. Report worsening pain, fever, or new neurological symptoms to your doctor.

faqs.question: Is treatment necessary for asymptomatic muscle cysts?
faqs.answer: Asymptomatic cysts found incidentally may not require immediate treatment; some calcify and involute spontaneously. However, brain MRI is mandatory to exclude neurocysticercosis, and a specialist should assess whether treatment is warranted.

faqs.question: Why are corticosteroids given with cysticercosis treatment?
faqs.answer: When cysticerci die during anthelmintic therapy, they trigger a granulomatous inflammatory reaction that can cause pain, swelling, and (if brain involvement is present) cerebral oedema. Short-course corticosteroids blunt this reaction and improve symptoms.

faqs.question: Is muscular cysticercosis common?
faqs.answer: An estimated 2.5-3 million people worldwide have symptomatic muscular or subcutaneous cysticercosis. Seroprevalence in endemic areas reaches 10-20%, but most infected people are asymptomatic.

faqs.question: Where is muscular cysticercosis most common?
faqs.answer: Muscular cysticercosis is most prevalent in rural areas of Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia — regions where open defecation coexists with free-roaming pig husbandry.

faqs.question: Can you die from muscular cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: Isolated muscular cysticercosis very rarely causes death. Mortality is associated with co-existing neurocysticercosis, particularly cisternal or intraventricular disease causing hydrocephalus or vasculitis.

faqs.question: Is muscular cysticercosis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Cysticercosis cannot be transmitted from person to person through casual contact. Only ingestion of Taenia solium eggs from faecal contamination causes infection. Intestinal taeniasis (tapeworm) can spread eggs to others.

faqs.question: Does cysticercosis cause cancer?
faqs.answer: No. Cysticercosis is a parasitic infection, not a malignancy. Cysts may be misdiagnosed as soft tissue tumours on imaging, but biopsy and serology confirm the diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cysticercosis and taeniasis?
faqs.answer: Taeniasis is an intestinal tapeworm acquired by eating undercooked pork containing cysticerci. Cysticercosis is the tissue infection from ingesting tapeworm eggs from another person's faeces. Taeniasis carriers are the source of cysticercosis in others.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from muscular cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms usually improve within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment. MRI evidence of cyst resolution appears 3-6 months after therapy. Most patients make a full functional recovery.

faqs.question: Can cysticercosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Re-infection is possible in endemic areas without improved sanitation and pig management. Recurrence of treated cysts is uncommon if the full course was completed. Brain MRI should be repeated at 3-6 months to monitor for new lesions.

faqs.question: Should family members be tested for cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: Household contacts of a cysticercosis patient should be screened for taeniasis (stool sample for proglottids or copro-antigen) and have clinical assessment for symptoms. Serological testing of contacts is debated.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with muscular cysticercosis?
faqs.answer: Light activity is encouraged. Strenuous exercise involving the affected muscle group should be avoided for 2-4 weeks during treatment-related inflammation. Normal activities can resume once pain resolves.

metaDescription: Muscular cysticercosis from Taenia solium larvae in muscle: symptoms, albendazole treatment, MRI diagnosis, prevention. Medically reviewed guide.

sources.title: García HH, Gilman RH, Gonzalez AE, et al. 2001. Albendazole and cysticercosis. N Engl J Med 344(11):795-800.
Nash TE, Singh G, White AC, et al. 2006. Treatment of neurocysticercosis: current status and future research needs. Neurology 67(7):1124-7.
WHO. 2020. Taenia solium cysticercosis fact sheet. World Health Organization.
